threat_description: Bitcoin ECDSA transaction hijacking: P2PK (Pay-to-Public-Key) outputs in Bitcoin expose the secp256k1 public key directly on-chain — these UTXOs are immediately quantum-vulnerable via Shor's algorithm. P2PKH and P2WPKH addresses initially expose only the public-key hash but become quantum-vulnerable after their first spend (when the public key is revealed in the signature). The combined quantum-exposed surface includes early Satoshi-era P2PK coins and any address that has ever transacted. Correction (audit 2026-05-18, verdict=misleading): Distinguish P2PK addresses (pubkey permanently exposed in UTXO, directly quantum-vulnerable) from P2PKH/P2WPKH addresses (only quantum-vulnerable after the first outbound transaction reveals the pubkey). The $718B figure needs a specific source and methodology.
crypto_at_risk: secp256k1 ECDSA, P2PK UTXOs (immediate quantum exposure), P2PKH/P2WPKH addresses post-first-spend
pqc_replacement: P2QRH (BIP-360) proposal with ML-DSA and SLH-DSA

threat_description: Blockchain quantum vulnerability: Ethereum uses ECDSA secp256k1 for transaction signing. Ethereum addresses are keccak256-hashes of the public key — the public key is exposed once an account broadcasts its first outbound transaction (revealed in the ECDSA signature), creating direct Shor-attack exposure for any address that has ever transacted. The Ethereum Foundation roadmap discusses account abstraction (EIP-4337, EIP-7560) as a path toward quantum-safe wallets, but no finalized EIP mandates a PQ migration timeline. Correction (audit 2026-05-18, verdict=inaccurate): Remove EIP-7885 — verify the correct EIP number or reference Ethereum Foundation quantum research discussions. Replace 'P2PK outputs' (Bitcoin concept) with 'accounts with at least one outbound transaction (which expose the public key in the ECDSA signature)'.
crypto_at_risk: ECDSA secp256k1 transaction signing, public-key exposure on first outbound transaction, BIP-32/39/44 HD wallet derivation
pqc_replacement: Account Abstraction (EIP-4337), Verkle Trees, quantum-resistant signature schemes

threat_description: Hash function collision resistance halved by quantum algorithms: Grover's algorithm reduces the pre-image resistance of SHA-256 from 256-bit to ~128-bit effective security. Quantum collision attacks (Brassard-Høyer-Tapp / BHT) reduce SHA-256 collision resistance from 128-bit to ~85-bit effective security. SHA-1 (already classically broken) is fully negligible post-CRQC. Quantum security of a hash for collision/preimage is determined by output size: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-384, and SHA3-512 (larger outputs) provide substantially improved quantum margins; SHA3-256 has the same 256-bit output as SHA-256 and therefore equivalent quantum resistance. HMAC-SHA-256 is less affected due to keying.
crypto_at_risk: SHA-1 (broken classically), SHA-256 collision resistance, MD5, RIPEMD-160, Merkle trees using SHA-256
pqc_replacement: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-256, SHA3-512 for collision-sensitive applications; SLH-DSA hash chains use SHA-256/SHA-512 by design
main_source: NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev 6 / NIST IR 8545
